justification: All 7 required rules identified (plus 2 extras). However, invariant classification is systematically wrong: accrual rate, 3-day advance notice, medical leave retroactive exception, and carryover cap are ALL marked invariant: true when they should be false (configurable policies, not structural invariants). That's 4 misclassifications — the agent failed to distinguish company/HR policy from true domain invariants, which is a core competency being tested. Ambiguities are well-surfaced (contract end date inclusivity, 5-day window calendar vs working days, partial month accrual, pending request reservation). Acceptance criteria are excellent — 11 Given/When/Then scenarios plus 7 edge cases, far exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence at 82 is reasonable given the depth of analysis, though the agent should have been less confident given it didn't question its own invariant classifications.

observations.text: All 7 required business rules identified plus 2 additional valid rules (valid date range, manager approval)
Excellent ambiguity surfacing: contract end date inclusivity, 5-day retroactive window (calendar vs working days), partial month accrual, pending request balance reservation
11 detailed Given/When/Then acceptance criteria plus 7 edge cases — far exceeds the 4 minimum
Strong ubiquitous language section with precise definitions
Rejection reasons section adds defensive value by flagging implementation pitfalls
Accrual rate (1.5 days/month) marked invariant: true — this is a configurable HR policy, not a structural constraint
Carryover cap (10 days) marked invariant: true — this is a policy that varies by company/jurisdiction
3-day advance notice marked invariant: true — this is a waivable company policy, not a domain invariant
Medical leave retroactive exception marked invariant: true — this is an HR policy rule, not a structural invariant
No explicit distinction between labor-law-adjacent rules (balance integrity, contract boundary) and HR policy rules (accrual rate, carryover cap) — a key rubric requirement
Nearly all rules marked invariant: true (8 of 9), approaching the fail criterion of uniform true classification. Only 'manager approval' was marked false.
